summary: Design the Sybil-resistant link-local token source for polleneus, select the best, then red-team all v0.3 new primitives with it baked in
logs: Selected token source: Reciprocity Hourglass: diversity-gated witnessed-relay credit, priced by a self-anchored sequential-PoSW + ZK-nullifier token (WRR spine x Hourglass primitive)

candidates.name: Hourglass: self-anchored Proofs-of-Sequential-Work spent as zero-knowledge nullifier tokens
candidates.angle: COMPUTE-MINTED. A token is a fresh, self-issued Proof-of-Sequential-Work (Cohen-Pietrzak hash-based PoSW + Fiat-Shamir) over a SECRET self-chosen statement. There is no issuer keypair to blind (Privacy Pass cannot apply directly because nobody signs). Instead the proof itself IS the bearer value, and unlinkability is obtained not by blinding an issuer signature but by NEVER revealing the proof: the BLE handshake spend is a non-interactive zero-knowledge argument that "I hold a valid PoSW over a commitment I can open," releasing only a one-time nullifier. Rate is bounded by sequential device-hours (wall-clock x cores-don't-help), so the honest ceiling is device-COUNT, not humans. I deliberately chose hash-based PoSW over a Wesolowski/Pietrzak class-group VDF for buildability (R6): a phone has SHA/BLAKE hardware but no fast class-group arithmetic, and PoSW needs no trusted setup or group of unknown order.
candidates.howMintingWorks:
PRIMITIVE: Cohen-Pietrzak Simple Proofs of Sequential Work (eprint 2018/183), made non-interactive by Fiat-Shamir. Sequentiality assumption: a depth-N hash chain over a depth-robust DAG cannot be shortcut by parallelism; computing the labeling takes ~N sequential hash steps even with many cores. Verification is cheap: sample t challenge leaves, check t*log(N) Merkle/labeling hashes (t=n=~16-21 => a few hundred hashes, sub-millisecond).

MINT (offline, background, while phone is in pocket):
1. Device draws secret serial s (256b) and blinding r (256b). Computes statement chi = H("polleneus-mint" || com) where com = Commit(s; r) (Pedersen on Ristretto255, or a hash commitment).
2. Runs the PoSW labeling of difficulty N over chi: builds the DAG labels L_i = H(chi || i || parent-labels), Merkle-roots them to phi.
3. Fiat-Shamir: challenge set {q_1..q_t} = H(chi || phi). Opens those t leaves with Merkle paths => proof Pi. N is calibrated so one token ~= one device-CPU-hour (tunable; see attacks). Battery: this is the cost knob, not a bug (R7).
A minted token is the tuple tok = (s, r, com, phi, Pi). It is self-validating: anyone can check Pi proves N sequential steps were done over chi=H(com). No issuer, no network (R4).

SPEND (inside the BLE handshake, consumes one relay slot):
The naive spend "show tok" is FULLY LINKABLE (phi/Pi are deterministic in s) and re-spendable. So we never show it. The relay slot purchase is a Schnorr-style NIZK (Fiat-Shamir over the handshake transcript) proving:
  "I know (s,r,phi,Pi) such that com=Commit(s;r) AND PoSW.Verify(H(com),phi,Pi)=accept,"
and revealing exactly one derived value: nullifier nf = H("nf" || s || epoch_anchor) where epoch_anchor is the neighbor's session nonce from the handshake (see serverlessOffline). The neighbor checks the NIZK (cheap: PoSW verify is hash-only; the ZK wrapper proves a hash-relation, so use a STARK-friendly hash like Poseidon/Rescue inside the circuit, or a Bulletproofs/sigma composition over the commitment + a hash-chain membership proof) and that nf is unused this session. Accept => one relay slot granted. The proof and nullifier carry no identity and never enter the blob, so the message soup stays uniform (invariant 2/3/4).
candidates.sybilResistance: R1: Minting one token costs N sequential hash steps ~= one device-hour of real wall-clock time that CANNOT be parallelized away (that is the whole point of *sequential* work vs ordinary PoW). A single device therefore mints at most ~1 token/hour regardless of how many cores or how much money is thrown at one machine. Buying more parallel hardware does not raise a single device's token rate; it only raises the device COUNT (honest limit). This is strictly better than memory-hard PoW (Argon2/RandomX), which IS parallelizable and lets one rich actor mint in bulk; sequentiality is exactly the property that turns "spend money" into "spend wall-clock-per-device." Verification asymmetry is huge (hours to make, microseconds to check), satisfying R6.
candidates.farmResistance: R2 is where compute-minting genuinely SHINES and where pairing-based schemes fail. Because tokens are minted by COMPUTE ALONE with no pairing/attestation/co-location step, there is no "pair two phones to mint" loophole to exploit: a device farm of K phones in one room mints exactly K tokens/hour - identical to K phones spread across the world. Co-location confers ZERO advantage; the farm gets no multiplier. The ONLY way to mint faster is more devices each grinding sequentially, and each device's output is capped by physics (clock speed * sequentiality). A 100-phone farm = 100 tokens/hr = the cost of 100 phones running hot. Contrast proof-of-relay/tit-for-tat, which a farm games trivially by self-relaying; Hourglass has no relationship to relaying at mint time, so no self-dealing. The residual farm threat is purely economic (botnet/cloud), addressed under attacks.
candidates.anonymityImpact: R3: Strong, and this is the part requiring the ZK wrapper (the honest cost). ACQUISITION: minting is a purely local computation touching no peer and no network - it emits nothing on the wire, so it cannot be observed or correlated at all. SPENDING: the spend is a NIZK that reveals only (a) that *some* valid PoSW-token exists and (b) a nullifier nf=H(s||epoch_anchor). Because the proof is zero-knowledge over s, two spends by the same device are unlinkable (different tokens => independent s => independent nf; even the SAME logical session can't link distinct tokens). The nullifier is a uniformly-random-looking hash carrying no identity. Crucially I do NOT use the raw PoSW proof as the token (that would be linkable: phi is a deterministic function of s). UNIFORMITY: the handshake-token bytes are fixed-length pseudorandom (a sigma/STARK transcript + a 256b nullifier), so a real spend, a cover spend (spend-to-nowhere), and a relayed spend are byte-indistinguishable (invariant 3). One leak to flag: spend TIMING/RATE is observable to the immediate neighbor (how many slots you buy), a metadata channel mitigated by mandatory cover-spending.
candidates.serverlessOffline:
R4/R5: No issuer (the proof is self-certifying), no internet, no global clock, no convergence, no shared ledger. The classic killers - "fresh challenge from a beacon" and "global double-spend set" - are both avoided:
(a) CHALLENGE FRESHNESS without a clock: a self-chosen chi invites PRECOMPUTE (grind tokens for years in advance). We don't need a global beacon; we bind spend-validity to a LOCAL, per-neighbor, per-session epoch_anchor = the random session nonce both phones contribute in the BLE handshake (commit-reveal of two 128b nonces => neither side predicts it). The nullifier nf=H(s||epoch_anchor) is recomputed at spend time from the *already-minted* secret s and the fresh anchor, so old tokens stay spendable (good - compute is not wasted) BUT each token yields a fresh, unpredictable nullifier per session. This is purely local entropy; no beacon, no clock (R5).
(b) DOUBLE-SPEND without global state: detection is LOCAL exactly as the spec demands - each phone keeps a per-neighbor, per-session set of seen nullifiers and grants <= R slots. There is no global nullifier tree and none is needed: the rate-limit is per-neighbor-per-session by design (spec v0.3 §9). A token CAN be re-spent against a *different* neighbor - that is fine and intended, since the limit is per-edge relaying, not global supply. Partitions/short contacts are tolerated: a spend is one round-trip, state is ephemeral session-local, nothing to converge (R6).
candidates.incentiveEffect: R7: Mixed, honestly. Direct effect: minting costs battery/CPU and is decoupled from relaying, so it does not directly rebuild the relay reciprocity that tit-for-tat gives - a pure compute-minter could mint then refuse to relay. To align with WANTING to relay, layer a REBATE: a phone that relays a slot for a neighbor issues that neighbor a fresh small "relay-credit" anonymous token (a cheap chained PoSW seeded by the spent nullifier, or a one-show credit), so honest relayers passively accumulate spendable tokens and rarely need to grind. Net effect: heavy relayers are token-rich (good for the commons); leeches must burn CPU/battery to participate, an incentive-compatible tax on non-contribution. The danger to flag: if rebates are too generous they reintroduce a self-pairing farm loophole (mint-by-relaying), so rebate value must stay strictly below mint cost. Compared to RLN, no staking/slashing and no global epoch - the forbidden dependencies stay gone.
candidates.buildability: R6: The mint (PoSW) is trivially buildable - just a hash function (BLAKE3/SHA-256), DAG labeling, Merkle tree; no class groups, no RSA modulus, no trusted setup, runs in background on any phone, verify is a few hundred hashes (sub-ms, negligible battery for the verifier). The hard/expensive part is the ZK SPEND wrapper: proving "PoSW.Verify accepts" in zero-knowledge means proving a hash relation, which is costly in generic SNARKs. Practical path: (1) use a STARK-friendly hash (Poseidon/Rescue) INSIDE the PoSW so the spend proof is a small STARK/Bulletproof (~tens of KB, ~10-100ms to verify on a phone), or (2) avoid proving PoSW-in-ZK entirely by a hybrid: reveal phi/Pi to a one-time ephemeral "checker" role... - rejected, leaks linkability. So (1) is the build. Token-bytes are fixed-length, fitting BLE MTU with fragmentation. Battery: mint is the deliberate cost (calibrate N so a token ~ minutes-to-an-hour); verify and spend-proof are cheap. Calibrating N across heterogeneous phones (slow vs fast SoC) is a real engineering wrinkle (see attacks).

attacks.title: Botnet / cloud-farm minting (the real R1/R2 break)
attacks.mechanism: Sequentiality caps PER-DEVICE rate but not device COUNT. An attacker renting 10k cloud VMs or running a botnet mints 10k tokens/hour - each VM is a legitimate 'device.' Compute-minting reduces Sybil to 'how many independent sequential cores can you rent,' which money still buys (just less efficiently than parallel PoW). In a stadium of 50k phones this may be tolerable; against a funded adversary it is the dominant attack. Mitigation is only economic (raise N) which trades off honest-user battery (R7) - a genuine, unresolved tension.

attacks.title: Precompute / stockpiling
attacks.mechanism: Because chi is self-chosen with no global freshness, an attacker grinds tokens for months before an event, then floods. The epoch_anchor binding makes each token re-spendable but only ONE slot per neighbor-session, so a stockpile buys breadth (many neighbors) not depth (overwhelming one edge). Stockpiling raises the effective farm size but does not bypass the per-edge R limit. Residual risk: an attacker who arrives with a huge stockpile is indistinguishable from many honest relayers - acceptable given the per-edge cap, but it means the scheme bounds PER-NEIGHBOR abuse, not total tokens in existence.

attacks.title: Sequentiality assumption failure / fastest-clock advantage
attacks.mechanism: PoSW security rests on no shortcut to a depth-N hash chain. An adversary with a faster single-thread (overclocked ASIC clocking one hash chain faster, or a future break) mints faster than honest phones. Also heterogeneous hardware means N calibrated for a budget phone lets a flagship mint several tokens in the same wall-time. No fix without a global difficulty oracle (forbidden); must accept a constant-factor advantage to the fastest serial hardware.

attacks.title: ZK-wrapper soundness / implementation bugs
attacks.mechanism: The whole unlinkability + double-spend story depends on a correct, sound NIZK proving 'PoSW accepts over an opening of com' and a correctly bound nullifier. A bug (unsound proof, malleable nullifier, missing domain separation between neighbors) lets a token be re-spent or forged with no compute. This is the largest software-correctness risk and the part least like off-the-shelf code.

attacks.title: Nullifier cross-neighbor replay / under-binding
attacks.mechanism: If epoch_anchor is not strongly bound (e.g., a malicious neighbor replays another's session nonce), an attacker could make the same nf accepted in two contexts or pre-grind nullifiers. Mitigated by commit-reveal of BOTH nonces and including both phones' ephemeral pubkeys in the anchor, but a Sybil neighbor that fakes many sessions can still extract many slots from one victim (this is a relay-DoS, partially the point of the R cap).

attacks.title: Spend-rate metadata leak
attacks.mechanism: Although token bytes are uniform, the COUNT and TIMING of slot purchases a device makes to a given neighbor is observable to that neighbor and weakly fingerprints heavy senders. Mitigated by mandatory cover-spends (spend-to-nowhere at a baseline rate) so real vs cover traffic is indistinguishable, at a battery/token cost.

attacks.title: Battery griefing / asymmetric DoS
attacks.mechanism: Mint is expensive for the honest minter but verify is cheap for everyone, so the asymmetry favors defenders for spends. The reverse risk: forcing honest phones to mint (via the relay-rebate economy) drains batteries; a low-battery phone effectively can't participate, mildly excluding the resource-poor - an equity/availability concern rather than a security break.

candidates.name: Witnessed Relay Reciprocity (WRR): pairwise delivery-acks minted into diversity-gated, self-spending relay credit
candidates.angle: RELAY-CREDIT-RECIPROCITY / proof-of-relay tit-for-tat. You earn the right to have YOUR blobs relayed by demonstrably relaying OTHERS' blobs, where "demonstrably" is a pairwise, intra-contact, identity-free delivery-acknowledgement (a stripped-down HOPR Proof-of-Relay key-half swap), and credit is gated by the DIVERSITY of distinct peer-radios you serve, not the raw count of relays — so the farm/self-pairing loophole is starved at the source.
candidates.howMintingWorks: THREE-PART MECHANISM. (A) The proof-of-relay kernel (pairwise, no routing, no blockchain). When neighbor U hands neighbor D a blob B during a BLE contact, they run an immediate, symmetric receipt swap modeled on HOPR's Proof-of-Relay but with everything global stripped out. Concretely: D, on ACCEPTING B into its soup, returns a Delivery-Ack DAck = a BLAKE2s MAC over (H(B) ‖ session_nonce ‖ epoch_salt) keyed by a per-contact ephemeral key derived from the BLE pairing's shared secret (X25519 ECDH between the two random per-session keypairs already exchanged when the link came up). DAck proves "a distinct radio that I was physically next to accepted this specific blob in this session." It carries NO identity (the per-session key is fresh and discarded) and is unlinkable across sessions. To make it a *fair* exchange despite Pagnia-Gärtner (strong fair exchange is impossible without a TTP), U and D do NOT try to atomically swap relay-for-proof; instead they piggyback the receipt on the SAME reconciliation round that already moves the symmetric-difference of their blob sets (§8). The swap is gradual/interleaved per-blob (like GRUZ one-bit-at-a-time gradual release): each accepted blob in the diff yields one DAck back, so a cheater who walks away mid-round loses at most ONE blob's worth of receipt — fairness violation is bounded to one slot, the BitTorrent tit-for-tat property. (B) Minting credit from receipts — diversity-gated. A phone collects DAcks over a neighbor-local epoch (epoch = a sliding ~5-min window keyed to its own monotonic counter, NOT a global clock). It does NOT get one credit per DAck. Instead it locally computes a DIVERSITY ESTIMATE over the distinct per-session-pubkeys that issued DAcks this epoch (a HyperLogLog / cuckoo cardinality sketch of issuer-keys, plus a count of distinct PHY-observed radios from the link layer). Credit minted this epoch = min(novelty_served, distinct_radios)^(density-adaptive curve), i.e. credit is a CONCAVE function of how many *different* neighbors you actually delivered novel blobs to, sharply saturating once the same small set of peers keeps acking you. Each unit of credit is materialized as a single-use unlinkable RELAY-TOKEN: token = (s, π) where s is a fresh random 256-bit secret and π is a tiny self-VDF proof (a few hundred ms Wesolowski squarings over s, reusing the §9 VDF machinery) that makes each token cost real sequential wall-clock time to fabricate — so even a node that somehow forges diversity still pays VDF-seconds per token. The token is self-issued (no issuer needed): its validity is "presenter proves knowledge of s whose VDF proof π checks AND s has not been seen by THIS verifier this session." (C) Spending. To get its own blob relayed, the originator (or any forwarder) spends a token INTO the BLE handshake exactly as spec §9 already prescribes: it reveals s (or a Schnorr proof of knowledge of s to keep s reusable-by-nobody but unlinkable) and the neighbor checks π once (cheap, ms), records s in its per-session seen-set, and grants one relay slot. Tokens are consumed in the handshake, NEVER embedded in the blob, so the soup stays uniform. Verification cost: one VDF verify (ms) + one cuckoo membership check. Net loop: relay others' novelty to diverse neighbors -> collect DAcks -> mint diversity-gated, VDF-priced tokens -> spend tokens to relay your own.
candidates.sybilResistance: R1 partial-to-good. One actor cannot cheaply mint unlimited tokens for THREE stacked reasons. (1) VDF floor: each token embeds sequential squarings, so a single device mints at a hard rate of ~1 token / VDF-period regardless of cores or mains power (sequentiality collapses the ASIC/GPU advantage to a small constant, per §9). N devices = N parallel streams, so Sybils are bounded by *device count*, not free. (2) Diversity gate: credit is concave in distinct-radios-served, so spinning up many virtual identities on ONE radio yields almost nothing — the diversity sketch sees one PHY radio and one cluster of self-issued session keys. This is the key departure from naive proof-of-relay, which a single node games by claiming relays. (3) Receipts must come from OTHER radios: a DAck is keyed by a shared secret from a real BLE ECDH with a physically-present peer, so you cannot mint your own DAcks without a second real radio in range. The honest residual: this is NOT a true per-actor quota (impossible under invariants 3/4, per §12.3); it is the "physically expensive + locally bounded shadow." A wealthy actor with K real radios in K different locations gets K streams — but that already costs O(K) hardware deployed across O(K) places, which is exactly the project's stated honorable bound (O(crowd-size) hardware).
candidates.farmResistance: R2 — this is where WRR earns its keep, and it is the angle's hardest problem. A naive proof-of-relay is TRIVIALLY farmed: put 50 phones in a backpack, have them relay cover blobs in a ring, each "proves" it relayed, everyone mints. WRR defeats the *ring* but not the *spread-out farm*, and is honest about that. Defenses: (1) Diversity gate (primary). Credit = concave(distinct radios served). A co-located ring of 50 farm phones forms a clique — each phone only ever serves the same 49 peers, so the distinct-radio estimate saturates at ~49 and credit per phone is capped at the concave ceiling, NOT 49x linear. The farm gets sublinear return on hardware. This is the "collocation-edge" insight from Sybil-defense literature (Quercia/SybilBelief, the Mason Test): Sybil devices can only form collocation edges with the attacker's OWN devices, so an internal clique is detectable as low external diversity. (2) Novelty requirement. A DAck only counts toward minting if the blob delivered was NOVEL to the receiver (it was in the symmetric-difference, §8 reconciliation gives this for free). A farm relaying the same recycled cover blobs in a loop produces ZERO novelty after the first pass — the sketch of "distinct novel blobs served" flatlines. To keep minting, the farm must keep injecting genuinely new blobs, which each cost a VDF (§9 mint-cost) — so manufacturing fake novelty is itself rate-limited and expensive. (3) Density-adaptive price (§9). In a dense real crowd token price is high (throttle); a farm trying to look dense by packing radios RAISES its own local density reading, which RAISES the token price it must pay to spend — self-defeating. HONEST LIMIT: a farm spread across the venue at true crowd density, each radio serving genuinely distinct real strangers, is INDISTINGUISHABLE from honest heavy relayers and SHOULD mint — but that farm is also genuinely improving the mesh, so R7 turns the attack into a contribution. The unfixable residual farm is "K real radios at K real distinct locations," which is the O(crowd-size) hardware bound, stated plainly.
candidates.anonymityImpact: R3 — strong, with one caveat. Tokens carry no identity: s is fresh random, π is a function of s only, and spending reveals a Schnorr PoK of s (or just s) with no link to any keypair. Acquisition is unlinkable: DAcks are MAC'd under per-session ephemeral keys that are discarded, so two DAcks from the same physical peer in two sessions are uncorrelatable. Spending is unlinkable to acquisition: a minted token shares no bits with the DAcks that justified minting it (credit is an aggregate count, tokens are freshly sampled). Uniform with cover: cover blobs run the identical relay/DAck/mint/spend loop (cover traffic earns and spends credit exactly like real traffic), so a relayer cannot tell whether a spent token is buying a real or a cover blob's slot — the soup and the handshake both stay uniform. The token spend is in the handshake, never in the blob (preserves invariant 3/4). CAVEAT (honest): the DAck swap adds a small, observable intra-contact protocol exchange. A PHY-fingerprinting sniffer (device-linkage ≈ 1.0, per §10) can already label radios, so it could observe "radio X issued ~N DAcks this contact" — this leaks a relaying-INTENSITY signal per radio, not message content or social graph. It must be made uniform: pad DAck exchange to a fixed per-contact count (issue exactly R DAck-slots per neighbor, real-or-dummy, matching the §9 R-blobs-per-neighbor cap), so the wire shows a constant receipt cadence. With that padding, acquisition leaks nothing beyond "this radio runs the app and relays," which is already assumed.
candidates.serverlessOffline: R4/R5 — clean fit, this is the mechanism's best property. NO issuer: tokens are self-minted; their cost is local (VDF squarings) and their justification is local (DAcks you hold). NO internet: every step is a BLE pairwise exchange. NO global clock: epochs are each phone's OWN sliding monotonic-counter window; nothing requires two phones to agree on time (a DAck's epoch_salt is the issuer's local salt, opaque to others — used only so the issuer can age out its own seen-set). NO global state / tree / convergence: there is no Merkle tree of valid tokens (the RLN trap), no global double-spend ledger, no consensus. Double-spend is prevented PURELY LOCALLY: a verifier records spent s-values in its per-session seen-set (a cuckoo filter), so a token can be spent at most once PER NEIGHBOR PER SESSION — which is exactly the granularity §9 wants (R blobs per neighbor per session). A token CAN be re-presented to a different neighbor, but that just buys a relay slot there, which is the intended behavior (you spend credit to propagate; each hop costs a fresh spend, compounding against floods). No convergence is needed because correctness is defined link-locally: "did THIS neighbor already see THIS s THIS session?" Works identically in a total blackout.
candidates.incentiveEffect: R7 — strongest of all the token-source candidates; this is the angle's whole point. The token source IS relaying others' novel traffic to diverse peers — the exact behavior the mesh needs. It directly attacks the relay tragedy-of-the-commons: a selfish node that hoards battery and refuses to relay earns NO credit and therefore cannot get its own messages out, so rational self-interest now pulls toward relaying. It composes with §9's density-adaptive auto-knobs: near the percolation cliff (sparse) token price -> ~0 and DAcks are easy, so relaying is cheap to monetize precisely when the mesh most needs relays; in a dense crowd price rises, throttling floods. It rewards SPREADING novelty to MANY distinct peers (the diversity gate), which is exactly optimal gossip dissemination behavior — you are paid to be a good epidemic spreader, not a hoarder. Mild risk: it could over-incentivize chasing novel blobs / diverse contacts, but since cover traffic counts equally and the reward saturates concavely, there is no runaway. Net: converts the anti-abuse token from a tax into a reward for prosocial relaying.
candidates.buildability: R6 — buildable, modest cost, partition-tolerant. Piggybacks entirely on infrastructure spec v0.3 already mandates: the BLE reconciliation round (§8) carries DAcks in the same diff exchange (near-zero extra bytes: one 16-byte MAC per accepted blob, and they're capped at R per neighbor); the VDF machinery (§9) is reused for token cost; the cuckoo filters (§6 seen-record) are reused for per-session spent-s tracking and diversity cardinality. Verify cost: one VDF verify (~ms, cheap by Wesolowski design) + one cuckoo lookup per spent token — trivial battery. Mint cost: a few hundred ms of squarings per token, batchable in background (like §10's VDF pre-generation, which is safe here because anonymity comes from the gate+Poisson mixing, not token timing). Partition/short-contact tolerant: a 2-second brush yields a few DAcks and progresses reconciliation (rateless); credit accrues incrementally; tokens are durable local state that survives going offline. No new wire format beyond a fixed-size DAck slot and a token-spend field in the handshake — both must be fixed-size/non-fingerprintable per §8. Weak-phone concern: the diversity sketch + VDF on low-end Android is the main thing to benchmark (open item, same as §5's puncturable-encryption benchmark).

attacks.title: Spread-out device farm at true crowd density (the unfixable residual)
attacks.mechanism: An attacker deploys K real radios across K distinct locations in the venue, each serving genuinely distinct real strangers. Each radio sees real diversity, real novelty, pays real VDF, and legitimately mints K independent credit streams. The diversity gate, novelty check, and density price all PASS because the behavior is genuinely honest relaying. This is indistinguishable from K honest heavy users and cannot be stopped. Mitigation is only the economic/physical bound: it costs O(K) hardware physically deployed across O(K) places — the project's stated honorable O(crowd-size) bound — and R7 means each such radio is actually improving the mesh, partially defanging the attack. Label: bends nothing, but is a true limit on R1/R2.

attacks.title: Receipt-farming clique / self-pairing ring
attacks.mechanism: M phones in one bag relay recycled blobs in a ring, each issuing DAcks to the others to mint credit without serving the real crowd. Defeated by the stack: (a) diversity gate saturates at the clique size with a concave ceiling, not linear; (b) novelty check zeroes out after the first pass since recycled blobs aren't in anyone's symmetric-difference; (c) faking novelty requires minting new blobs, each costing a VDF; (d) packing radios raises local density, raising the price they must pay to spend. Residual: the clique can still mint the concave-ceiling amount once per novelty-injection — a small constant per VDF-period per device, i.e. bounded, not zero.

attacks.title: DAck withholding / unfair-exchange grief (Pagnia-Gärtner attack)
attacks.mechanism: A node accepts your novel blobs (improving its own soup) but walks away before issuing the matching DAcks, denying you the credit you earned — strong fair exchange is provably impossible without a TTP, so this CANNOT be fully closed. Bounded by the GRUZ-style gradual/interleaved swap: receipts are exchanged per-blob within the reconciliation round, so an aborter steals at most ONE blob's worth of credit before you stop feeding it (tit-for-tat). Repeated aborters are fast-droppable via the §8 'neighbor whose round never completes' hook — a link-local, identity-free penalty. Cannot escalate to systematic theft.

attacks.title: Token replay across many neighbors (intended) vs. true double-spend (prevented locally)
attacks.mechanism: Because there is no global ledger (R5 forbids it), a token can be presented to neighbor A, then later to neighbor B. This is INTENDED — each hop must cost a spend, so re-presenting buys a slot at B legitimately and floods still pay per-hop-per-neighbor. The only thing prevented is spending the SAME token TWICE to the SAME neighbor in the SAME session, caught by the per-session spent-s cuckoo set. The residual: a token's total 'value' is bounded by the number of distinct neighbors you meet, which is exactly the per-neighbor-per-session metering §9 wants. No global double-spend protection is attempted or needed.

attacks.title: Diversity-sketch poisoning via forged session keys
attacks.mechanism: A single radio rapidly rotates many fresh per-session pubkeys to inflate its distinct-issuer count in a victim's diversity sketch (or its own, to mint more). Mitigated by binding the diversity estimate to PHY-observed distinct radios (the link layer / radiometric count, which a single transmitter cannot inflate — device-linkage ≈ 1.0 cuts BOTH ways and here it HELPS) AND by rate-limiting new BLE associations per unit time (a single radio can only complete so many full ECDH+reconciliation handshakes per second). Residual: a sophisticated attacker spoofing PHY characteristics could still inflate, but that requires SDR-grade hardware per fake identity — back to the O(K) hardware bound.

attacks.title: Credit hoarding / pre-mining burst
attacks.mechanism: An attacker relays honestly for a long quiet period, banks a large token reserve, then dumps it in a burst flood. Unlike a pre-mined PoW pool this does NOT amortize to zero: each spend still costs a per-neighbor slot and compounds against propagation (a blob must win a token spend at EVERY hop at EVERY neighbor), and the density-adaptive price spikes during the burst the attacker creates. The VDF floor also caps how fast the reserve could be built. Effect is a bounded, self-throttling burst, not an unbounded flood — strictly better than the e-cash/PoW pre-mining failure mode that killed those candidates here.

attacks.title: DAck-intensity side channel
attacks.mechanism: A PHY-fingerprinting sniffer counts how many DAck slots a labeled radio uses per contact, inferring relaying intensity (not content, not social graph). Mitigated by padding to a fixed R DAck-slots per neighbor per session (real-or-dummy), matching the §9 cap, so the receipt cadence on the wire is constant. Residual leak after padding is only 'runs the app and relays,' already in the threat model.

candidates.name: Proof-of-Encounter: Diversity-Weighted Mutual Co-Signed Encounter Tokens (KVAC-based)
candidates.angle: PROOF-OF-ENCOUNTER. Tokens are minted only by witnessed physical co-presence with DIVERSE distinct peers. Each genuine, distance-bounded encounter lets the two phones mutually co-sign a batch of single-use relay tokens for each other. Anti-farm resistance comes not from "an encounter happened" (a farm can manufacture those) but from a privacy-preserving DIVERSITY ledger: a token is only mint-able when the new peer is sufficiently *novel* relative to the set of peers you have recently met, measured by a local, identity-free Bloom-style sketch of peer ephemeral commitments. A device that only ever meets the same N farm-mates saturates its diversity sketch and stops minting; a person walking through a crowd keeps meeting novel peers and keeps minting. The token itself is a keyed-verification anonymous credential (algebraic-MAC / KVAC, Chase et al.) so issuance and spending are unlinkable, carry no identity, and are locally verifiable in the next BLE handshake with no issuer and no global state.
candidates.howMintingWorks:
PRIMITIVE CHOICE. Each identity holds a self-generated KVAC issuer keypair (algebraic MAC over a prime-order group, Ristretto255; Chase-Meiklejohn-Zaverucha CCS'14 / Chase-Perrin-Zaverucha CCS'19 non-interactive KVAC). "Token issued by me" = an algebraic MAC under my secret key x over a hidden attribute (a random serial s the holder picks blindly). I verify tokens issued by ME (symmetric: verifier knows the key) -> serverless, O(1) verify, ~sub-ms on a phone. Crucially the token a peer spends in MY handshake must be one I co-signed, so I am always the verifier of my own key -> no PKI, no issuer.

ENCOUNTER + MUTUAL CO-SIGN (one BLE contact, ~4 round trips):
1. Distance-bound the contact. Run a UWB time-of-flight ranging exchange if both phones have UWB (iPhone 11+/Pixel 6+), else a BLE RTT challenge-response (rapid-bit exchange, NCC-Group-style time-of-flight bound). This caps the relay/wormhole radius to a few meters -> a remote farm cannot inject a "phantom peer." (Honest: BLE-only ToF is weak; see attacks.)
2. Freshness beacon. Each side derives a per-contact ephemeral commitment C = H(domain || nonce_self || rounded-coarse-epoch) where the coarse epoch comes from the gossip-median soft clock already in spec (§9, no synchronized clock needed; ~10-min granularity). No persistent ID is revealed; C is single-use.
3. Diversity gate (local, see below). Each side checks: is the *other* side's commitment-class novel against my recent-peers sketch? If not novel enough, mint count for this contact is throttled toward 0 (still relay, still cover; just no new tokens).
4. Mutual blind issuance. A picks a fresh random serial s_A, blinds it (Pedersen commitment), and runs the KVAC blind-issuance protocol so B computes a MAC on s_A *under B's key* without learning s_A. Symmetrically B gets a MAC on s_B under A's key. Each side receives a batch of n = f(diversity, density) tokens (n typically 1-8). So a token = (s, MAC_peerkey(s)) where the holder knows s but the issuer never saw it.
5. Done. Tokens are stored locally, unlinkable to the encounter.

SPENDING (in a later handshake, §9):
When A later meets relay-peer V and wants a relay slot, A must spend a token that V issued. A runs the KVAC *presentation*: a zero-knowledge proof of knowledge of (s, MAC_Vkey(s)) plus the serial s revealed (or a unique pseudonym derived from s for double-spend detection within V's session). V checks the MAC under its OWN key (it issued it) and that s is unseen this session. Counts toward R-per-neighbor-per-session. Token is burned. NOTHING about this touches the blob -> soup stays uniform.

So the economy is: you earn relay-credit with peer V *by having physically met V (or met someone V trusts, see transitivity below) in a diverse encounter*, and you spend it to relay through V. Tokens are peer-scoped (issued under V's key), which is exactly what §9 wants (rate is per-neighbor-per-session).

TRANSITIVITY OPTION (to avoid needing to have met *every* future relay peer): two variants. (a) Strict peer-scoped (above): you can only spend with peers you literally encountered. Strong anti-Sybil, weak liquidity. (b) Epoch-anchored "bearer" variant: instead of self-keys, the network agrees (via the same gossip-median soft consensus) on a rolling coarse-epoch tag T; tokens are KVAC credentials over attribute (s, T) and ANY node verifies a token by checking a *threshold* co-signature: the token must carry MACs from k>=2 distinct issuers whose issuer-commitments the verifier has independently witnessed in the soup (issuer-commitments flood as ordinary cover blobs). This makes tokens bearer-spendable network-wide while still requiring k independent real co-signers. I recommend shipping (a) for the high-assurance core and offering (b) as the liquid mode, clearly flagged as weaker (bends R5 slightly via the epoch tag).
candidates.sybilResistance: R1 partial-to-good. Minting requires a *mutual* co-sign with a DISTINCT peer, and a token spent at V must be issued by V. So one actor with one device cannot self-mint: a token you sign for yourself verifies under your key, not under the relay peer's key, so it is worthless for spending elsewhere. To get spendable tokens you need other real keyholders to co-sign for you. Creating a fake identity is free (no server/account, R6), BUT a fake identity only yields tokens that VERIFY UNDER THE FAKE'S KEY -> only useful to relay through that same fake. Sybils are therefore bounded by *device count physically present and distance-bounded*, not by number of identities (you can mint 10^6 identities but each still needs a real radio in the room to be co-signed and to be a useful relay peer). This is exactly the §9 ambition: "Sybils bounded by device count, not human count." It does NOT achieve a true human-quota (no proof-of-personhood offline), and it inherits the VDF's residual: N real radios = N token streams. Honest framing: this raises the floor from "free unlimited tokens" to "tokens cost a real, distance-bounded, diverse physical contact per device."
candidates.farmResistance:
R2 is the heart of this angle. A naive "encounter = token" scheme is trivially farmed: 50 phones in a closet pair round-robin (50*49 encounters) and mint endlessly. Defense = DIVERSITY-WEIGHTED minting, not encounter-counting:

1. SATURATING NOVELTY SKETCH. Each phone keeps a decaying counting Bloom sketch of the commitment-classes of peers met in the recent soft-epoch window. The number of tokens granted for an encounter is n = base * novelty(peer) * (1 - saturation), where novelty drops to ~0 if the peer's commitment-class is already dense in your sketch, and saturation rises as your *distinct-peer* count in the window approaches a cap K_div. A closet of 50 phones: after each phone has met the other 49 once, every subsequent pairing is non-novel -> mint rate collapses to ~0. The farm's *total* lifetime mint is bounded by ~K_div distinct peers, NOT by encounter count. To keep minting, the farm must keep introducing genuinely-new physical devices -> cost scales linearly with real hardware, which is the §9 hardware-bound philosophy.

2. SPATIAL DIVERSITY WITHOUT LOCATION. The coarse-epoch in the commitment C means re-meeting the same peer in a *different* epoch is partially novel (people do re-meet) but a static farm meeting the same mates across epochs gains far less than a mobile user meeting new mates each epoch. We deliberately do NOT use GPS/location (R3) — diversity is measured purely over the *set of distinct peer commitments x epochs*, which a co-located farm cannot inflate without more distinct real devices.

3. DISTANCE BOUNDING (UWB/ToF) stops a "virtual farm" of one device emulating many remote peers over a relay/wormhole: a phantom peer must answer a few-nanosecond ToF challenge from within meters. (BLE-only ToF is weak; UWB strong.)

4. RATE OF NOVELTY CAP. Even a mobile attacker walking a stadium is bounded: tokens minted per unit time <= base * (rate of meeting genuinely-novel real devices), which for a single human in a crowd is just... a normal person's mint rate. That is the point: the honest user and the best single-device attacker mint at the SAME rate. A farm only beats that by buying more real, mobile, distinct radios. Residual: a "festival farm" — a person walking K_div*phones through a real dense crowd can mint K_div times a normal user (each phone independently meets novel peers). Mitigated by per-physical-radio PHY-fingerprint correlation being out of scope; honestly a real weakness (see attacks).
candidates.anonymityImpact: R3 well-handled by construction. (1) Issuance is BLIND: the issuer co-signs a serial s it never sees (Pedersen-committed, KVAC blind issuance), so the issuer cannot later recognize the token. (2) Spending is a ZK presentation revealing only a one-time serial/pseudonym, not which encounter produced it, and unlinkable across spends (KVAC presentation is multi-show-unlinkable). (3) Tokens carry NO identity and never enter the blob — they live only in the BLE handshake, so the soup stays bit-for-bit uniform (real == cover == relayed), preserving invariant 3. (4) The encounter commitment C uses single-use nonces and coarse epochs, not stable IDs, so the *act of minting* does not build a social graph. CAVEAT (honest): the encounter itself is a physical co-presence event; an external observer with PHY fingerprinting (device-linkage ~1.0 per §12) sees two radios near each other regardless of our crypto. We do not claim to hide that two devices were co-present from a USRP-equipped adversary; we hide *which* token came from *which* encounter and we add nothing identifying to the wire beyond what BLE already leaks. The diversity sketch is LOCAL-ONLY and never transmitted, so it leaks nothing. Net: cryptographic acquisition+spending unlinkability is strong; physical co-presence metadata is a pre-existing BLE/PHY limitation, not worsened by this design.
candidates.serverlessOffline: R4/R5 strong, this is the design's best fit. NO issuer: every node is its own KVAC issuer and the verifier of its own key (symmetric keyed verification — the whole reason KVAC fits offline, vs Privacy Pass which needs a central issuer). NO internet ever. NO global clock: the only time input is the coarse soft-epoch from the gossip-median clock already in §9 (~10-min granularity, tolerant of skew); strict mode (a) doesn't even need that for verification, only for diversity weighting. NO global state / tree / convergence: double-spend detection is purely LOCAL — verifier V checks the serial against the tokens it has seen IN THIS SESSION (same scope as the existing R-per-neighbor-per-session counter, §9). A token spent twice in the same session is caught; cross-session/cross-node double-spend is intentionally NOT prevented because that would require global state — instead the per-neighbor-per-session R cap bounds the damage (a double-spent token just buys one more slot at one more neighbor, which is already capped). This deliberately accepts bounded double-spend to avoid the global-convergence requirement that "killed RLN and offline e-cash here." Works in a total blackout: two phones touching can mint; a phone with a token pocket can spend it at any peer that co-signed it (mode a) or any peer (mode b) with zero infrastructure.
candidates.incentiveEffect: R7 GOOD — arguably the design's standout property, and better than VDF/PoW here. Tokens are earned by ENCOUNTERING peers and are spent to RELAY. The receive-before-originate gate (§10) already requires relaying to originate; this closes the loop: to keep relaying (and thus to keep your own messages moving and to keep originating) you need tokens, and you get tokens by being physically present and meeting people — which you are anyway at a protest/stadium/campus. So the mechanism rewards exactly the behavior the network needs (showing up, staying in the crowd, carrying the soup) and it costs nothing extra in attention. Mutual co-signing is symmetric and cooperative (tit-for-tat-flavored, like BitTorrent's choke algorithm but for proximity): both peers benefit from each contact, so there is positive incentive to complete handshakes rather than free-ride. Contrast with VDF, which burns battery for nothing the user values. One risk: it could discourage relaying when your token pocket is empty (you met few people) — mitigated by the density-adaptive auto-knob setting token price ~0 when the mesh is sparse/near the percolation cliff (§9), so scarcity of encounters lowers the price exactly when generosity matters most.
candidates.buildability: R6 moderate, mostly buildable. KVAC (algebraic MAC over Ristretto255) is real, standardized-ish, and fast: issuance and presentation are a handful of scalar mults; verify is sub-ms on a phone; tokens are ~100-200 bytes; this is the same family used in Signal's group-credential system (production-proven on phones). Blind issuance + ZK presentation are well-specified (Chase et al.). Battery: minting is a short BLE exchange already happening on contact; no VDF-style sustained compute -> *cheaper* than the VDF path for the token economy. Partition/short-contact tolerant: a single ~4-RTT BLE contact suffices to mint; no long-lived session needed. The HARD parts, honestly: (1) Distance bounding — UWB is great where present but coverage is partial; BLE-only ToF is genuinely weak (NCC Group showed BLE relay defenses are bypassable), so on BLE-only devices the wormhole/phantom-peer defense degrades to "weak" and we must say so. (2) The decaying counting Bloom diversity sketch needs careful parameterization (window size, K_div, decay) and is the main tuning risk. (3) Bounded double-spend is a deliberate correctness compromise that must be clearly bounded by the R cap. (4) Mode (b) threshold co-signing across issuers adds the gossip-median epoch dependency. Overall: shippable for mode (a) on UWB-capable phones; mode (a) on BLE-only and mode (b) carry real caveats.

attacks.title: Mobile device farm (festival-walker)
attacks.mechanism: A single human carries K_div mobile phones through a genuinely dense, diverse crowd. Each phone independently meets novel real peers and mints at the full honest rate, so one operator gets K_div x normal mint throughput. Diversity gating does NOT stop this because the peers ARE genuinely novel. Only PHY-fingerprint correlation (out of scope, §12) could tie the K phones to one carrier. This is the residual hardware-bound the whole §9 philosophy accepts: cost scales linearly with real radios, but it does scale, not blocked.

attacks.title: BLE-only wormhole / phantom-peer injection
attacks.mechanism: Without UWB, the time-of-flight bound is weak (NCC Group: BLE proximity auth is relay-bypassable at link layer). Two colluding devices relay the mint handshake over the internet/long-range radio so a remote 'peer' appears local, manufacturing diverse encounters with devices that were never co-present. Defeats the spatial-diversity assumption on BLE-only handsets. Mitigation: require UWB ToF for full mint rate; BLE-only contacts mint at a steeply discounted rate; flag the weakness.

attacks.title: Sketch-poisoning to fake novelty
attacks.mechanism: An attacker's device deliberately rotates its per-contact commitment C every contact (using fresh nonces) so that to a victim it always looks like a NEW peer, letting a small farm mint repeatedly against the same physical victim. Mitigation: novelty is judged against the *peer's* presented commitment-class, but C is freshly chosen by the peer each time -> partial defense only. Stronger: bind C to a slowly-rotating per-epoch value so within an epoch a peer cannot present many distinct classes; bend toward requiring the SAS/ephemeral_pk continuity. Residual leak vs. anonymity if binding is too tight (tension with R3).

attacks.title: Bounded double-spend across neighbors/sessions
attacks.mechanism: Because there is no global double-spend ledger (deliberate, R5), a token can be spent once per (neighbor, session). An attacker re-spends the same token at many neighbors and across sessions, getting more relay slots than 'earned'. Bounded by the R-per-neighbor-per-session cap (§9): the extra value is exactly one slot per neighbor per session, which is already the network's hard cap, so the blast radius is small — but it does erode the 'single-use' framing. Honest: tokens are single-use *per verifier-session*, not globally single-use.

attacks.title: Encounter-coercion / rubber-hose minting
attacks.mechanism: An adversary forces targets to perform mint handshakes to harvest tokens issued under the targets' keys. Yields only tokens spendable as relay-credit with the coerced parties (mode a) — low value, and physically expensive. Mode (b) bearer tokens make this worse (network-wide spendable), another reason mode (b) is flagged weaker.

attacks.title: Diversity-cap DoS on honest mobile users
attacks.mechanism: Setting K_div and decay too low starves legitimate highly-social users (who meet many people) of mint capacity, or conversely too high re-opens the closet-farm. Misparameterization is a real operational risk; density-adaptive auto-knobs (§9) help but add tuning surface.

attacks.title: Co-sign refusal / griefing
attacks.mechanism: A peer completes the encounter but refuses to issue the mutual MAC (takes your co-sign, gives nothing). Since issuance is mutual and ordered, a fair-exchange / simultaneous-release step is needed; without it a griefer denies tokens. Mitigation: tit-for-tat — withhold your co-sign until theirs verifies, accept that the worst case is 'no token minted', which is not catastrophic (you just don't gain credit).

candidates.name: Quorum-Witnessed Tokens (QWT): threshold-local blind issuance bound to ambient co-presence
candidates.angle: THRESHOLD-LOCAL-ISSUANCE. A token is a Coconut/Pointcheval-Sanders attribute credential over a user-chosen random secret s, blind-signed by a k-of-n threshold of physically nearby peers acting as ad-hoc witness-issuers. No single device is "the issuer"; the right to mint is distributed across whoever happens to be co-present. Sybil-resistance comes not from a registry but from the cost of assembling a *diverse local quorum* whose members each independently rate-limit issuance and each cryptographically attest that they observe the *same physical environment* as the requester. The novel contribution over vanilla Coconut is (a) anonymous, identity-free quorum formation over BLE, and (b) binding every partial signature to an unforgeable per-epoch ambient co-presence beacon plus per-witness diversity budgets, which is what defeats the bring-your-own-n device farm.
candidates.howMintingWorks:
PRIMITIVES. (1) Coconut threshold credentials [Sonnino et al., arXiv:1802.07344] = Pointcheval-Sanders signatures with blind issuance + client-side aggregation of t partial sigs into one short credential, re-randomizable and unlinkable across shows even if all authorities collude on blindness. (2) A non-interactive distributed key is NOT needed: Coconut authorities use independent keypairs and the aggregation is a Lagrange interpolation in the exponent over their public verification keys, so any ad-hoc set works without a DKG ceremony. (3) An "epoch beacon" e = a coarse ambient fingerprint (see serverless section).

SETUP (no server). Every phone holds its own Coconut authority keypair (x_i, y_i) -> vk_i = (g2^x_i, g2^y_i), generated locally at install. There is NO global key. A token is verifiable by ANY phone that has *seen the witnesses' vk_i out-of-band-or-gossiped* — but to keep verification serverless we instead make tokens self-describing: the credential is accompanied by an aggregated verification key derived from the specific quorum, and the spender proves in zero-knowledge that the quorum satisfied the public local policy (k distinct witnesses, valid epoch beacon). Verification = 2 pairings, independent of k.

MINT PROTOCOL (one neighbor-local session, ~k short BLE contacts):
 1. Requester picks random secret s and blinding; forms an ElGamal/Pedersen commitment cm to s plus a Coconut "lambda" = (cm, encrypted s under ephemeral ElGamal key, NIZK that they are consistent). This is the blind request; witnesses never learn s.
 2. Requester binds the request to the current epoch beacon e by including H(e) as a public attribute inside the credential and a NIZK that the e it used equals the e the witness independently measured (range/equality proof over the fuzzy-extractor output of the ambient fingerprint).
 3. Requester broadcasts lambda over BLE to nearby peers using a fresh ephemeral pairing identity (sealed-sender; §4/§6 invariants).
 4. Each willing witness i runs Coconut BlindSign: checks the NIZK, checks that H(e) matches its own locally-measured beacon (co-presence test), checks its own per-epoch issuance budget b_i (it will issue at most B partial sigs per epoch), and returns a blinded partial credential sigma_i' together with vk_i and a short proof "issued-under-policy".
 5. Requester unblinds each sigma_i' -> sigma_i, verifies, and once it has k from *distinct* witnesses (distinctness enforced by the diversity rule below), aggregates via Lagrange into a single credential sigma on s with aggregated vk.
 6. The TOKEN = (sigma, s, aggregated-vk, e). It is single-use because spending reveals a deterministic, unlinkable tag tau = H'(s)·(generator) — actually a Coconut "double-spend tag" / nullifier derived from s — that the spending neighbor records for this session.

SPEND (in the BLE relay handshake, never in the blob — keeps the soup uniform, invariant §2/§3): to use a relay slot with neighbor N, requester sends N a *show* of the token: a re-randomized sigma + NIZK that sigma is a valid credential over some s whose nullifier is tau, that e is within N's accepted epoch window, all WITHOUT revealing s or which witnesses signed. N checks 2 pairings + that tau is unseen this session + that the quorum-policy proof holds, then grants one relay slot and stores tau locally until session end. Because the show is re-randomized, two spends of two different tokens are mutually unlinkable, and a spend is unlinkable to its mint.
candidates.sybilResistance: R1. Minting one token requires k DISTINCT witnesses to each spend one unit of their per-epoch budget B. A single actor cannot mint by themselves: they would need k devices, and each device only has B units per epoch, so a lone actor with d devices can mint at most floor(d/k)·B tokens per epoch — linear in hardware, not free. The marginal cost of a token is "k device-budget-units," not "one signature." Crucially, the *honest* population also acts as witnesses for free, so an honest user surrounded by N>>k strangers mints freely while an isolated attacker is throttled to their own hardware. Sybil identities are cheap in the abstract but here an identity that cannot be physically co-present and cannot pass the ambient co-presence check (below) contributes zero issuing power. The scheme converts "cheap virtual identities" into "expensive physical witnesses." Honest-majority assumption is local and weak: we need only k honest-or-independent devices in radio range, not n/2 of the world.
candidates.farmResistance:
R2 — the hard part; this is where vanilla Coconut fails. A farm = attacker brings k+ devices co-located in one box. Three layers resist it:
 (a) AMBIENT CO-PRESENCE BEACON. The epoch beacon e is a fuzzy-extractor output over a *high-entropy ambient channel* that an attacker cannot privately reproduce or predict: e.g. the multipath/CSI + RSSI fingerprint of the live RF environment plus an audio-spectral hash of ambient sound [ResearchGate Ambient Audio; Ranger/UTA RF noise pairing; arXiv:1808.03320 co-presence survey]. A device farm in a Faraday-quiet van shares ONE environment, so all its devices produce the SAME e — which is fine for passing the co-presence check but is exactly what makes them DETECTABLE/limited: the diversity rule (below) and the global rate that any single e can back is bounded. More importantly, e is *unpredictable and unforgeable ahead of time*, so an attacker cannot pre-compute tokens for future epochs; tokens are bound to the live ambient state and self-destruct with the epoch (ties to invariant §5 TTL).
 (b) WITNESS DIVERSITY / DECORRELATION. Each partial sig carries a *witness-diversity attestation*: a commitment to that witness's own independently-sampled ambient sub-fingerprint plus a distance-bounding nonce. The aggregator's quorum-policy NIZK must prove the k witnesses' fingerprints are pairwise DECORRELATED beyond a threshold (different antenna positions/multipath) — co-located farm devices on the same chassis have near-identical CSI and fail decorrelation, so a farm cannot supply k *independent-looking* witnesses from one location. Real crowds (phones in pockets, meters apart, bodies attenuating) decorrelate naturally.
 (c) DISTANCE-BOUNDING ROUND. The BlindSign exchange includes a rapid challenge-response round (distance bounding [Kuhn; imec BLE distance-bounding]) so a witness can refuse partials to a prover that is *not physically within d meters*, blocking the "relay one real witness to many fake requesters" amplification.
HONEST CAVEAT: layers (a)/(b) are heuristic physical-layer defenses, not cryptographic hardness. A sophisticated farm with k spatially-separated antennas + motion actuators across a real room CAN manufacture decorrelated fingerprints. The honest claim is: farming cost scales with *physical spatial diversity and time*, not with virtual identities — it raises the bar from "free script" to "engineered multi-antenna rig per k tokens/epoch," which for a stadium-scale adversary is a meaningful but not absolute deterrent.
candidates.anonymityImpact: R3 — strong, this is Coconut's home turf. Blindness: witnesses sign a commitment to s and never learn s, so issuance reveals nothing linkable; even a full coalition of all witnesses cannot link an issued partial to a later show (Coconut blindness holds against unbounded adversaries even if verifier colludes with authorities). Unlinkability: each spend re-randomizes the credential, so mint!=spend linkage and spend!=spend linkage are both broken. No identity on the wire: quorum formation uses fresh ephemeral BLE identities; the token carries no issuer identity, only an *aggregated* vk that is a function of the (anonymous) quorum's public keys — and to avoid even that leaking "which k phones," the spend proves quorum validity in zero-knowledge against the *public local policy* rather than disclosing vk_i. Uniformity with cover: the token is consumed in the HANDSHAKE, never embedded in the blob, so the flooded soup stays uniform (invariant §2/§3) — cover, real, and relayed blobs remain identical. LEAK SURFACE (honest): the epoch beacon e is a coarse location/time fingerprint; if e were shown in the clear it would deanonymize to "someone at this place-time." We therefore never reveal e, only a NIZK that e is within the verifier's accepted window — but the *set of acceptable e* still narrows the anonymity set to "people in this dense gathering this epoch," which is acceptable given invariant §7 (scope is one dense gathering anyway) but is a real, bounded leak.
candidates.serverlessOffline: R4/R5 — fully serverless, no global clock, no convergence. No issuer, no DKG, no registry: each phone is its own Coconut authority with a locally-generated keypair; "issuance authority" is whoever is nearby and willing. The epoch beacon e replaces a global clock with PURELY LOCAL, opportunistic time: e is derived from the live ambient RF/audio environment sampled over a coarse window (say 5 min). Two co-present phones independently measure correlated raw ambient data and run a fuzzy extractor to agree on the SAME e with no communication and no synced clock — this is exactly the ambient-audio/RF key-agreement result [Ambient Audio; UTA RF noise; CSI key extraction]. No convergence/tree/CRDT: a token is valid the instant k local partials are aggregated; there is no global set of authorities to agree, no Merkle tree, no gossip-to-consensus. Verification is local and stateless except the per-session nullifier set (small, bounded by R blobs/neighbor/session, discarded at session end). Self-destruct (§5): e is embedded as a signed attribute and tokens are only accepted while the verifier's own ambient window still matches e (absolute, locally-judged TTL); past-epoch tokens are cryptographically dead because no current verifier will have a matching e window — optional crypto-shred by rotating the fuzzy-extractor helper data per epoch. Works in total blackout: BLE + microphone + radio noise need no internet, no infrastructure, no satellite time.
candidates.incentiveEffect: R7 — net positive and self-correcting. Being a WITNESS (issuing partial sigs) is the act that mints other people's relay tokens; we make witnessing the SAME action as relaying by paying witnesses: a phone that issues a partial sig to a neighbor earns a small token-credit itself (a partial-sig receipt redeemable toward its own future mint), so the population that helps others relay is exactly the population that earns the right to relay. This directly counters the relay tragedy-of-the-commons: hoarding (refusing to witness) starves you of credits and thus of your own relay slots, while generous witnessing within the per-epoch budget B is rewarded. Because B caps each witness, you cannot earn unboundedly by spamming partials. The alignment: token wealth flows to those physically present AND cooperative, which is precisely the behavior the DTN needs. RISK (honest): if witnessing is too cheap it invites the farm; if too costly people won't witness and the token supply collapses in sparse crowds — B must be tuned to crowd density, and there is no global tuner (must be a fixed conservative constant or locally adapted to observed neighbor count, which itself is a mild manipulation surface).
candidates.buildability: R6 — buildable but heavy at the edges. CRYPTO COST: Coconut/PS partial sign = a couple of group ops on BLS12-381; aggregation = k Lagrange-scaled point mults; verification = 2 pairings (constant in k) — all feasible on a modern phone in low ms, and verification cost (the hot path, run per relay handshake) is cheap and battery-modest. NIZKs (blind request, quorum-policy, equality-of-e) are Schnorr-style sigmas, sub-ms. BLE FIT: mint needs k short contacts in one session; tolerant of partitions because you can collect partials opportunistically over the whole session window and aggregate late (Coconut partials are independent — no interactive round between witnesses, unlike FROST which needs a nonce-commit round; this is why PS/Coconut beats FROST here for ad-hoc disconnected witnesses). HARD PARTS: (1) ambient fuzzy extraction (audio+CSI) is real engineering — needs reliable same-e agreement across heterogeneous phones, which is the least mature component and the main build risk. (2) BLE distance bounding needs tight timing the BLE stack doesn't natively expose well; likely approximated by RSSI+challenge-response, which is weaker. (3) verifying witness-diversity (CSI decorrelation) requires raw radio access that mobile OSes restrict. BATTERY: continuous ambient sampling for the beacon is the dominant cost; mitigated by sampling only during active sessions. Verdict: the threshold-blind-signature core is production-grade and cheap; the physical co-presence layer is a research-grade prototype.

attacks.title: Engineered multi-antenna farm (the residual pairing-mint loophole)
attacks.mechanism: Attacker builds a rig with k spatially-separated antennas + moving reflectors in a real room so the per-witness ambient sub-fingerprints decorrelate and pass the diversity check, while all k devices are under one control. Defeats the heuristic farm defense; mints floor(devices/k)*B tokens/epoch at engineering cost, not free. This is the honest ceiling of the scheme: physical-layer co-presence is heuristic, not hard.

attacks.title: Ambient-beacon replay / prediction
attacks.mechanism: If the ambient channel is low-entropy (quiet room, static RF), e becomes predictable or reproducible, letting an attacker pre-mint tokens or mint from outside the gathering by replaying a recorded/relayed ambient sample to their own farm. Mitigation: require minimum measured entropy before issuing; mix in a per-contact distance-bounding nonce so e alone is insufficient. Residual risk in sterile environments.

attacks.title: Quorum-collusion over-issuance
attacks.mechanism: k colluding real witnesses can mint tokens for each other freely up to k*B, and since they are genuinely distinct/decorrelated devices, no layer stops them. A coordinated bloc (e.g. an organized group at a protest) can out-mint and thus out-relay/out-spam honest users. No global state means no way to detect cross-session over-issuance. Inherent to k-of-n local trust; only B and k bound it.

attacks.title: Witness-budget exhaustion DoS
attacks.mechanism: Adversary floods nearby honest witnesses with blind-sign requests to burn their per-epoch budget B, so honest requesters in the area can no longer assemble a quorum (issuing power is depleted). Distance bounding limits radius but a co-located attacker can still drain neighbors. Mitigation: witnesses prioritize requesters that present partial-sig receipts (cooperators), turning it into a soft proof-of-relay gate.

attacks.title: Relay/wormhole on the distance-bounding round
attacks.mechanism: Classic relay attack: attacker tunnels the rapid challenge-response between a far-away real witness and a local fake requester to forge proximity (BLE timing is coarse, so the time-of-flight bound is loose). Lets an attacker borrow honest issuing power remotely. True distance bounding needs PHY-layer timing the BLE stack barely supports; RSSI fallback is spoofable with amplifiers.

attacks.title: Nullifier-set partition double-spend
attacks.mechanism: The single-use guarantee is enforced by each neighbor's LOCAL nullifier set for the session. With no global state (by design), the same token can be spent once per distinct neighbor / per session boundary. An attacker spends the same token across many neighbors or waits for session rollover to respend, getting more relay slots than the one the token represents. This is an accepted consequence of serverless/no-convergence (§5/§R5): tokens meter *per-neighbor-per-session*, not globally — abuse is bounded by R per neighbor but not globally bounded.

attacks.title: Anonymity-set narrowing via e-window
attacks.mechanism: Even though e is never revealed in clear, the verifier's accepted e-window pins the spender to 'present at this gathering this epoch.' Combined with side channels (BLE timing, contact graph), this can shrink the anonymity set, especially in a small crowd near the k/density floor. Bounded leak, aligned with §7 scope but real; worse as crowd thins.

attacks.title: Sparse-crowd liveness failure
attacks.mechanism: Not an attack but a failure mode an adversary can induce: if fewer than k willing/honest witnesses are in range (thin crowd, or attacker scares off participants), no tokens can be minted and the relay layer stalls. The same property that gives Sybil-resistance (need a real local quorum) makes the system fragile exactly when the gathering is small — the opposite of graceful degradation.

candidates.name: VOUCH: Social-Graph Blind-Vouched Relay Budgets (KVAC dispensers issued at out-of-band pairing)
candidates.angle: SOCIAL-GRAPH-VOUCHED. Your real out-of-band contacts each blind-sign you a small, finite, per-epoch budget of single-show relay tokens. Your total token budget is bounded by the number of distinct humans who have physically paired with you and chosen to vouch — so Sybil-minting is bounded by your real social degree, not by compute, time, or device count. The wire stays uniform because tokens are spent only inside the BLE handshake (a KVAC presentation), never in the blob; verification is local and symmetric.
candidates.howMintingWorks:
PRIMITIVE: a keyed-verification anonymous credential (KVAC) over an algebraic MAC (Signal/CMZ14-style; pairing-free, ~Ristretto255), used as an n-show "dispenser" credential a la Camenisch-Lysyanskaya "Clone Wars" n-times tokens.

GROUP KEY (no server): the polleneus "soup" for a given dense gathering shares ONE symmetric KVAC key sk, generated out-of-band when the gathering's seed group forms (e.g. organizers' phones do a Pedersen-DKG over BLE/QR so sk is t-of-n threshold-shared; any peer verifies with the public iparams, and t organizers can co-sign). sk never touches the wire. Every phone holding a share is simultaneously an issuer AND a verifier — issuer==verifier is exactly the KVAC regime, so no pairings, cheap on a phone.

VOUCHING / MINTING (out-of-band pairing, contact A vouches for B):
1. B picks a fresh dispenser secret s (scalar) and seed; commits C = Comm(s, seed) with a Pedersen commitment, sends C + a ZK proof of well-formedness to A over the OOB channel (NFC tap / QR / short-range BLE during the deliberate pairing).
2. A (who holds a KVAC share) issues a BLIND partial-MAC on the hidden attributes (s, seed, epoch_e, n=budget). With threshold sk, B collects t partials from t different contacts and aggregates — OR a single contact who holds a full share issues alone if sk is per-contact-replicated. A learns nothing about s (blind issuance) and never sees future tokens.
3. B now holds a dispenser credential cert = MAC_sk(s, seed, e, n). It authorizes deriving n single-show tokens for epoch e.

SPENDING ONE RELAY SLOT (inside the BLE handshake with neighbor N, per neighbor-local session):
- B picks token index i in [0,n). Computes a serial S_i = PRF_s(e, i) (deterministic in s,e,i) and a "double-spend tag" T_i = G·s + H·PRF_seed(e,i)·chal where chal is a challenge N draws from the local session transcript (Schnorr-style, the classic e-cash 2-equations trick).
- B sends N a presentation: a re-randomized proof that "S_i and T_i were derived from a valid dispenser cert MAC'd under the group key sk, for this epoch, with i<n" — a standard sigma-protocol show of a KVAC. NO identity, NO s, NO seed revealed.
- N verifies with its sk share (or public iparams), records S_i in this session's seen-serial set, and grants one relay slot. Spent: one token.

R (the relay cap) is enforced trivially: N just counts distinct accepted serials this neighbor-local session; after R it refuses. Detection is purely local set-membership — no global clock/tree/convergence.
candidates.sybilResistance:
R1: A single actor's token supply is capped at (number of distinct real humans who paired-and-vouched for them) x (per-voucher budget b) x (epochs they were vouched). To mint more tokens you must convince more distinct real people to physically tap you. There is no compute/PoW shortcut and no time-accrual shortcut: holding sk-share-free, a non-vouched phone can derive ZERO valid tokens because the dispenser cert is a MAC under sk it does not possess and cannot forge (EUF-CMA of the algebraic MAC). So Sybil identities are free to CREATE but worthless — each must independently earn vouches from real humans. The cost of N tokens grows linearly in real-world social effort, which is the intended Sybil tax.

Caveat: this bounds minting by social degree, NOT by uniqueness of humans. A charismatic abuser with 200 real contacts gets 200b tokens. That is acceptable for the dense-gathering threat model (you only need to out-cost a flooder relative to honest relayers, not achieve one-token-per-human).
candidates.farmResistance:
R2 is the hardest requirement for this angle and I do NOT fully solve it; I raise its cost and bound the damage. The pairing-mint loophole: an attacker buys 50 phones, pairs them with each other, and has them mutually vouch -> 50x50 self-issued budgets.

Defenses, in order of strength:
(1) sk-gating: only phones holding a valid sk-share can issue. Shares are handed out only by the gathering's seed group (organizers) to phones that themselves presented a vouch chain rooted in the seed group. A farm of cold phones holds NO share, so they cannot vouch for each other at all. The farm must first get ONE phone vouched into the web-of-trust by a real participant. This converts the attack from "free farm" to "one real social entry point, then internal multiplication."
(2) Bounded fan-out per share + decay: each share can issue at most F vouches per epoch, and a vouch's budget DECAYS with social distance from the seed group (depth d budget = b * rho^d, rho<1). A farm chained off one entry point gets geometrically shrinking budgets. This is the real cap on internal multiplication.
(3) Reciprocity asymmetry / proof-of-relay coupling (optional, see incentiveEffect): tie refresh to having actually RELAYED for others (tokens spent BY OTHERS that you carried), which co-located self-pairing phones cannot manufacture without real third-party traffic.

Honest verdict: a determined farm with one legitimate entry point still multiplies tokens internally up to the depth/fan-out budget; (2) bounds it to a finite, decaying pool rather than unlimited. R2 is BENT, not preserved.
candidates.anonymityImpact:
R3 spending: STRONG. A KVAC show is re-randomized and unlinkable — two spends of two different tokens from the same dispenser are cryptographically unlinkable, and a spend reveals neither s, seed, the issuing contact, nor which contact vouched. The serial S_i is a PRF output (pseudorandom, carries no identity); the double-spend tag only deanonymizes you IF you reuse the same (e,i) — the "once concealed, twice revealed" property — which is a feature targeting cheaters, and even then it reveals s (a throwaway dispenser secret), not your real identity. Spending is uniform with cover: a cover-traffic handshake can present a cover token (a token spent on a cover blob) indistinguishably.

R3 acquisition: WEAKER, and this is the honest tension with invariants 4/6. Vouching is INHERENTLY a social-graph act: contact A learns "I vouched for somebody at this pairing." Blind issuance hides WHICH tokens B later spends, so A cannot link A's vouch to B's on-wire activity (good). But A knows B exists and was co-present at pairing time; and a threshold scheme requires B to collect t partials, so B's set of vouching contacts is known to those contacts collectively (not on the wire, but socially). The social graph is NOT on the wire (invariant 4 preserved) but it IS materialized out-of-band among contacts (invariant 6 strained: pairing now carries semantic "I trust you to relay," not just key exchange).
candidates.serverlessOffline: R4: No issuer server — issuance is peer-to-peer at pairing. R5 partial. The hard part is the EPOCH (e) without a global clock. Resolution: epochs are NOT wall-clock; they are gathering-local nonces. The seed group mints an epoch by broadcasting a signed epoch-beacon (random epoch_id + signed absolute TTL per invariant 5) into the soup; phones treat "the set of epoch-beacons I have heard, sorted by TTL" as the current epoch(s). A token is valid for any epoch-beacon currently live in your local view. This is eventually-consistent local state, NOT global convergence: two partitions may run different epochs; a token minted for epoch X is simply unspendable in a partition that never heard beacon X (graceful degradation, no fork-conflict, no merge needed). No global tree, no global clock, no convergence requirement — each verifier acts on its own heard-beacon set. Refresh without a server: when your epoch's TTL expires, you re-pair (or re-meet) any vouching contact who is reachable and they blind-issue a fresh dispenser for the new live epoch. If NO contact is reachable, your budget lapses — which is the explicit cost. So: contacts must be reachable AT REFRESH TIME (not at spend time). In a long blackout this means you need at least one vouching contact periodically co-present, which is realistic for stadium/protest/neighborhood scope.
candidates.incentiveEffect: R7: Can be made incentive-aligned, and this is the mechanism's best feature. Couple REFRESH to PROOF-OF-RELAY: a phone earns "relay receipts" by spending its slots carrying OTHER people's blobs; a contact's blind re-issuance for the next epoch requires presenting k anonymous relay-receipts (themselves KVACs issued by neighbors you relayed for, "thanks tokens"). Effect: to keep your budget you must actually relay for others -> directly fights the relay tragedy-of-the-commons (free-riders who only inject and never carry get no refresh and run dry). It also doubles as the R2 farm defense: self-paired farms generate no genuine third-party relay receipts. The naive version (pure social budget, no relay coupling) is incentive-NEUTRAL: it caps abuse but does nothing to encourage relaying, so I recommend the coupled variant.
candidates.buildability: R6: Buildable. KVAC show/verify over Ristretto255 is a handful of scalar mults — single-digit milliseconds and tens-of-bytes transcripts on a modern phone, well within a BLE handshake budget and battery. Issuance (blind MAC) is similarly cheap. No pairings (deliberately chose algebraic-MAC KVAC over BLS/Coconut for this reason). Partition tolerance: tokens are self-contained, no online check at spend time, so short contacts and partitions are fine — you only need a contact reachable at refresh. Threshold DKG among organizers is the heaviest part but runs once per gathering among a few phones, off the hot path. Storage: a dispenser is one cert + a small spent-serial set per active neighbor-session (bounded by R). Main cost: per-neighbor-session seen-serial set memory, trivially bounded by R x (number of concurrent neighbors).

attacks.title: Pairing-mint farm with one legitimate entry point
attacks.mechanism: Attacker gets one phone vouched by a real participant, then internally cross-vouches a farm. Bounded only by per-share fan-out F and depth-decay rho; with generous parameters the internal pool can still dwarf a single honest user. This is the core unsolved residue (R2 bent).

attacks.title: Voucher-side acquisition linkability / social-graph leak
attacks.mechanism: Vouching contacts collectively learn who asked them to vouch and when/where they were co-present. Blind issuance hides spend-linkage but not the existence of the vouch relationship. A coercive adversary who seizes phones can reconstruct the local web-of-trust from stored issuance logs (mitigate: crypto-shred issuance logs under invariant 5).

attacks.title: sk-share compromise / rogue issuer
attacks.mechanism: Anyone holding an sk-share can mint unlimited valid tokens (issuer==verifier symmetric key). One compromised organizer phone breaks Sybil-resistance entirely. Threshold t-of-n issuance mitigates (need t colluding shares) but verification still uses the shared key, so a leaked verification-capable key also lets forgery if the MAC is not strictly issuer-keyed. Must keep issuance threshold > expected compromise and rotate sk per epoch.

attacks.title: Token reuse / glitch deanonymization
attacks.mechanism: Reusing the same (e,i) across two sessions lets verifiers combine the two double-spend tags to recover s. This is intended (catches cheats) but a buggy client that legitimately resends after a dropped contact could self-deanonymize its dispenser secret. Needs careful idempotency/retry handling so honest retries reuse the SAME presentation transcript, not a fresh (e,i).

attacks.title: Epoch-beacon partition starvation / griefing
attacks.mechanism: Because epochs are local heard-beacon sets, an adversary who suppresses or floods epoch-beacons can make honest tokens unspendable in a region (DoS), or a partition that never hears a beacon strands legitimately-minted tokens. No global convergence means no automatic recovery until contacts re-meet.

attacks.title: Vouch-budget hoarding / resale
attacks.mechanism: A popular hub collects many vouches and could 'sell' relay capacity (spend on behalf of others). Tokens are bearer instruments; nothing binds a token to the human who earned the vouch, so capacity is transferable — a gray market in relay budget is possible, though capped by total social-graph supply.

attacks.title: Refresh-time contact-unreachability collapse
